attachment.content:
CRITICAL - Code Discovery Protocol:
1. ALWAYS use codebase-memory-mcp tools FIRST for ANY code exploration:
   - search_graph(name_pattern/label/qn_pattern) to find functions/classes/routes
   - trace_path(function_name, mode=calls|data_flow|cross_service) for call chains
   - get_code_snippet(qualified_name) for exact symbol source (precise ranges)
   - query_graph(query) for complex Cypher patterns
   - get_architecture(aspects) for project structure
   - search_code(pattern) for text search (graph-augmented grep)
2. Use Grep/Glob/Read freely for text, configs, non-code files, and
   always Read a file before editing it.
3. If a project is not indexed yet, run index_repository FIRST.

attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Current level: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman for: security warnings, irreversible action confirmations, multi-step sequences where fragment order risks misread, user asks to clarify or repeats question. Resume caveman after clear part done.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

content.text: Tell me how to run this project

addedBlocks:
## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- code-review:code-review: Code review a pull request
- caveman:cavecrew: Decision guide for delegating to caveman-style subagents. Tells the main thread WHEN to spawn `cavecrew-investigator` (locate code), `cavecrew-builder` (1-2 file edit), or `cavecrew-reviewer` (diff review) instead of doing the work inline or using vanilla `Explore`. Subagent output is caveman-compressed so the tool-result injected back into main context is ~60% smaller — main context lasts longer across long sessions. Trigger: "delegate to subagent", "use cavecrew", "spawn investigator/builder/reviewer", "save context", "compressed agent output".
- caveman:caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Ultra-compressed commit message generator. Cuts noise from commit messages while preserving intent and reasoning. Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤50 chars, body only when "why" isn't obvious. Use when user says "write a commit", "commit message", "generate commit", "/commit", or invokes /caveman-commit. Auto-triggers when staging changes.
- caveman:caveman-compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman-compress FILEPATH or "compress memory file"
- caveman:caveman-help: Quick-reference card for all caveman modes, skills, and commands. One-shot display, not a persistent mode. Trigger: /caveman-help, "caveman help", "what caveman commands", "how do I use caveman".
- caveman:caveman-review: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
- caveman:caveman-stats: Show real token usage and estimated savings for the current session. Reads directly from the Claude Code session log — no AI estimation. Triggers on /caveman-stats. Output is injected by the mode-tracker hook; the model itself does not compute the numbers.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# How to Run
2	
3	## Prerequisites
4	
5	| Tool | Required version |
6	|---|---|
7	| JDK | 21+ (Temurin recommended) |
8	| Maven | 3.6.0+ |
9	| Git | any recent version |
10	
11	Verify:
12	```bash
13	java -version      # should print openjdk 21
14	mvn -version       # should print Apache Maven 3.6+
15	```
16	
17	## Clone and build
18	
19	```bash
20	git clone https://github.com/graphhopper/jsprit.git
21	cd jsprit
22	mvn verify          # builds both modules + runs all tests
23	```
24	
25	Expected output (success):
26	```
27	[INFO] jsprit-core ................................ SUCCESS
28	[INFO] jsprit-analysis ........................... SUCCESS
29	[INFO] BUILD SUCCESS
30	```
31	
32	## Running tests
33	
34	```bash
35	# All tests (unit + integration)
36	mvn verify
37	
38	# Unit tests only
39	mvn test
40	
41	# Specific module
42	mvn test -pl jsprit-core
43	
44	# Single class
45	mvn test -pl jsprit-core -Dtest=VehicleRoutingAlgorithmTest
46	
47	# Single method
48	mvn test -pl jsprit-core -Dtest=VehicleRoutingAlgorithmTest#testMaxIterations
49	```
50	
51	## IDE setup
52	
53	### IntelliJ IDEA
54	1. **File → Open** → select the root `pom.xml` → open as project
55	2. Project SDK: set to JDK 21
56	3. IntelliJ will auto-import Maven modules (`jsprit-core`, `jsprit-analysis`)
57	4. Run any `*Test.java` with the green play button
58	5. `.editorconfig` is picked up automatically — ensure the EditorConfig plugin is enabled
59	
60	### VS Code
61	1. Install **Extension Pack for Java** (Microsoft)
62	2. Open the repo root folder
63	3. Java extension detects Maven project automatically
64	4. Use **Testing** panel to run JUnit 5 tests
65	
66	### Eclipse
67	1. **File → Import → Maven → Existing Maven Projects**
68	2. Select repo root, import both modules
69	3. Right-click test class → **Run As → JUnit Test**
70	
71	## Running a minimal solve (quick smoke test)
72	
73	Create a scratch file or run this in a test to verify the library works end-to-end:
74	
75	```java
76	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.algorithm.VehicleRoutingAlgorithm;
77	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.algorithm.box.Jsprit;
78	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.Location;
79	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.VehicleRoutingProblem;
80	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.job.Service;
81	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.solution.VehicleRoutingProblemSolution;
82	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.vehicle.VehicleImpl;
83	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.vehicle.VehicleTypeImpl;
84	import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.util.Solutions;
85	import java.util.Collection;
86	
87	// 1. Define vehicle type and vehicle
88	VehicleTypeImpl type = VehicleTypeImpl.Builder.newInstance("type")
89	    .addCapacityDimension(0, 10)
90	    .build();
91	VehicleImpl vehicle = VehicleImpl.Builder.newInstance("v1")
92	    .setStartLocation(Location.newInstance(0, 0))
93	    .setType(type)
94	    .build();
95	
96	// 2. Define jobs
97	Service s1 = Service.Builder.newInstance("job1")
98	    .setLocation(Location.newInstance(1, 2))
99	    .addSizeDimension(0, 2)
100	    .build();
101	Service s2 = Service.Builder.newInstance("job2")
102	    .setLocation(Location.newInstance(4, 5))
103	    .addSizeDimension(0, 3)
104	    .build();
105	
106	// 3. Build problem
107	VehicleRoutingProblem vrp = VehicleRoutingProblem.Builder.newInstance()
108	    .addVehicle(vehicle)
109	    .addJob(s1)
110	    .addJob(s2)
111	    .build();
112	
113	// 4. Solve
114	VehicleRoutingAlgorithm algo = Jsprit.createAlgorithm(vrp);
115	Collection<VehicleRoutingProblemSolution> solutions = algo.searchSolutions();
116	VehicleRoutingProblemSolution best = Solutions.bestOf(solutions);
117	
118	System.out.println("Cost: " + best.getCost());
119	System.out.println("Unassigned: " + best.getUnassignedJobs().size());
120	```
121	
122	## Configuring the algorithm
123	
124	```java
125	VehicleRoutingAlgorithm algo = Jsprit.Builder.newInstance(vrp)
126	    .setProperty(Jsprit.Parameter.ITERATIONS, "500")
127	    .setProperty(Jsprit.Parameter.THREADS, "4")             // parallel insertion
128	    .setProperty(Jsprit.Strategy.RADIAL_REGRET, "1.0")      // enable radial ruin
129	    .setProperty(Jsprit.Strategy.RANDOM_REGRET, "1.0")      // weight 1:1
130	    .buildAlgorithm();
131	```
132	
133	All `Parameter` and `Strategy` options are in `Jsprit.Parameter` and `Jsprit.Strategy` enums.
134	Default iterations: 2000. Default threads: 1.
135	
136	## Logging output
137	
138	jsprit uses SLF4J. Add a backend to your test classpath to see logs:
139	
140	```xml
141	<!-- pom.xml test scope -->
142	<dependency>
143	    <groupId>org.slf4j</groupId>
144	    <artifactId>slf4j-simple</artifactId>
145	    <version>2.0.16</version>
146	    <scope>test</scope>
147	</dependency>
148	```
149	
150	Or silence all jsprit output:
151	```xml
152	<dependency>
153	    <groupId>org.slf4j</groupId>
154	    <artifactId>slf4j-nop</artifactId>
155	    <version>2.0.16</version>
156	    <scope>test</scope>
157	</dependency>
158	```
159

file.content:
# How to Run

## Prerequisites

| Tool | Required version |
|---|---|
| JDK | 21+ (Temurin recommended) |
| Maven | 3.6.0+ |
| Git | any recent version |

Verify:
```bash
java -version      # should print openjdk 21
mvn -version       # should print Apache Maven 3.6+
```

## Clone and build

```bash
git clone https://github.com/graphhopper/jsprit.git
cd jsprit
mvn verify          # builds both modules + runs all tests
```

Expected output (success):
```
[INFO] jsprit-core ................................ SUCCESS
[INFO] jsprit-analysis ........................... SUCCESS
[INFO] BUILD SUCCESS
```

## Running tests

```bash
# All tests (unit + integration)
mvn verify

# Unit tests only
mvn test

# Specific module
mvn test -pl jsprit-core

# Single class
mvn test -pl jsprit-core -Dtest=VehicleRoutingAlgorithmTest

# Single method
mvn test -pl jsprit-core -Dtest=VehicleRoutingAlgorithmTest#testMaxIterations
```

## IDE setup

### IntelliJ IDEA
1. **File → Open** → select the root `pom.xml` → open as project
2. Project SDK: set to JDK 21
3. IntelliJ will auto-import Maven modules (`jsprit-core`, `jsprit-analysis`)
4. Run any `*Test.java` with the green play button
5. `.editorconfig` is picked up automatically — ensure the EditorConfig plugin is enabled

### VS Code
1. Install **Extension Pack for Java** (Microsoft)
2. Open the repo root folder
3. Java extension detects Maven project automatically
4. Use **Testing** panel to run JUnit 5 tests

### Eclipse
1. **File → Import → Maven → Existing Maven Projects**
2. Select repo root, import both modules
3. Right-click test class → **Run As → JUnit Test**

## Running a minimal solve (quick smoke test)

Create a scratch file or run this in a test to verify the library works end-to-end:

```java
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.algorithm.VehicleRoutingAlgorithm;
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.algorithm.box.Jsprit;
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.Location;
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.VehicleRoutingProblem;
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.job.Service;
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.solution.VehicleRoutingProblemSolution;
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.vehicle.VehicleImpl;
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.problem.vehicle.VehicleTypeImpl;
import com.graphhopper.jsprit.core.util.Solutions;
import java.util.Collection;

// 1. Define vehicle type and vehicle
VehicleTypeImpl type = VehicleTypeImpl.Builder.newInstance("type")
    .addCapacityDimension(0, 10)
    .build();
VehicleImpl vehicle = VehicleImpl.Builder.newInstance("v1")
    .setStartLocation(Location.newInstance(0, 0))
    .setType(type)
    .build();

// 2. Define jobs
Service s1 = Service.Builder.newInstance("job1")
    .setLocation(Location.newInstance(1, 2))
    .addSizeDimension(0, 2)
    .build();
Service s2 = Service.Builder.newInstance("job2")
    .setLocation(Location.newInstance(4, 5))
    .addSizeDimension(0, 3)
    .build();

// 3. Build problem
VehicleRoutingProblem vrp = VehicleRoutingProblem.Builder.newInstance()
    .addVehicle(vehicle)
    .addJob(s1)
    .addJob(s2)
    .build();

// 4. Solve
VehicleRoutingAlgorithm algo = Jsprit.createAlgorithm(vrp);
Collection<VehicleRoutingProblemSolution> solutions = algo.searchSolutions();
VehicleRoutingProblemSolution best = Solutions.bestOf(solutions);

System.out.println("Cost: " + best.getCost());
System.out.println("Unassigned: " + best.getUnassignedJobs().size());
```

## Configuring the algorithm

```java
VehicleRoutingAlgorithm algo = Jsprit.Builder.newInstance(vrp)
    .setProperty(Jsprit.Parameter.ITERATIONS, "500")
    .setProperty(Jsprit.Parameter.THREADS, "4")             // parallel insertion
    .setProperty(Jsprit.Strategy.RADIAL_REGRET, "1.0")      // enable radial ruin
    .setProperty(Jsprit.Strategy.RANDOM_REGRET, "1.0")      // weight 1:1
    .buildAlgorithm();
```

All `Parameter` and `Strategy` options are in `Jsprit.Parameter` and `Jsprit.Strategy` enums.
Default iterations: 2000. Default threads: 1.

## Logging output

jsprit uses SLF4J. Add a backend to your test classpath to see logs:

```xml
<!-- pom.xml test scope -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.slf4j</groupId>
    <artifactId>slf4j-simple</artifactId>
    <version>2.0.16</version>
    <scope>test</scope>
</dependency>
```

Or silence all jsprit output:
```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.slf4j</groupId>
    <artifactId>slf4j-nop</artifactId>
    <version>2.0.16</version>
    <scope>test</scope>
</dependency>
```

lastPrompt: Tell me how to run this project

content.text:
**Prerequisites:** JDK 21+, Maven 3.6.0+

**Build & run all tests:**
```bash
mvn verify
```

**Test subsets:**
```bash
mvn test                                          # unit tests only
mvn test -pl jsprit-core                          # core module only
mvn test -pl jsprit-core -Dtest=ClassName         # single class
mvn test -pl jsprit-core -Dtest=ClassName#method  # single method
```

No runnable binary — library only. Use the smoke test in [docs/how-to-run.md](docs/how-to-run.md) to verify end-to-end: build a `VehicleRoutingProblem`, call `Jsprit.createAlgorithm(vrp).searchSolutions()`, print `Solutions.bestOf(solutions).getCost()`.
